references.title: Capacity Tracing-Enhanced Course Recommendation in MOOCs

extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Course metadata, Responses for Cognitive Diagnosis, Knowledge Topics,
extracted.Initialization Method:
– MIRT static fit: estimate initial learner capacity uᵢ₀ and course difficulty dⱼ via maximum likelihood on ideal response qᵢⱼ


– Implicit response pᵢⱼ computed as geometric mean of completion ratio aᵢⱼ and relative duration tᵢⱼ/ Dⱼ
extracted.Updates to Recommendations:
– After each new interaction aᵢⱼ, update learner capacity uᵢ,ₜ by Δuᵢ = pᵢ,ₜ·λᵢ·ReLU(dⱼ – uᵢ,ₜ₋₁) (Eq 4), where λᵢ is per-learner growth rate

– Form embeddings: lᵢ,ₜ = concat(g(uᵢ,ₜ₋₁), l′ᵢ), cⱼ = concat(g(dⱼ), c′ⱼ), then predict with NCF or GRU by optimizing cross-entropy loss
extracted.Preprocessing Details: – Online setting: no offline train/test split for model fitting.– Compute pᵢⱼ via Eq 2; filter out records with missing aᵢⱼ or unknown
extracted.Evaluation: {Precision@10: 0.0715 - CT-E NCF, Precision@20: 0.1149 - CT-E NCF, Precision@40: 0.1827 - CT-E NCF, Hit_Ratio@10: 0.1605 - CT-E NCF, Hit_Ratio@20: 0.1290 - CT-E NCF, Hit_Ratio@40: 0.1025 - CT-E NCF}
extracted.Comments:
– Combines explainable MIRT capacities with CF.

– CT-E NCF yields highest Precision & Hit gains over all baselines.

– Capacity tracing correlates strongly with static MIRT estimates (ρ ≈ 0.79–0.99)
extracted.Limitations:
- No user study; offline only.
- Static dⱼ (no difficulty update).
- Assumes weak interactions suffice for pᵢⱼ.
- No specialized cold-start beyond MIRT init.

- Assumes completion ≈ mastery (may misestimate short sessions).

- No modeling of temporal drift beyond immediate updates.

annotations.text: Online features a very high dropout rate and, for that reason, tools for proper recommendations and tutoring are welcomed. Authors explore how item-response theory can be integrated into a recommender.

Authors use mutidimensional IRT and KT techniques to be able to diagnose a learner’s skills (so, first step is the creation of a knowledge model). Then, this model based on MIRT is used as input to a collaborative filtering algorithm for course recommendations.
Experiments on a real dataset show that approach not only improves effectiveness of MOOCs, but its explainability as well.
